voice_profile.register: Conversational, direct, unpretentious. Like a smart friend explaining something clearly, not a professor lecturing.
voice_profile.rhythm: Short declarative sentences. No throat-clearing. Opens essays mid-thought. Often starts with 'X seems Y. But.' Builds argument through concrete examples first, abstract principles after — the opposite of academic writing.
voice_profile.temperature: Cool to warm. Rarely heated. States hard truths calmly. Uses understatement for emphasis ('this seems like a problem' when it's a catastrophic problem).
voice_profile.humor: Dry. Absurd hypotheticals. Understatement. Never mocking, always illustrative. Often comes in parentheses.
voice_profile.conflict_style: Engages substantively. Doesn't dismiss critics — takes their position seriously and shows where it fails. Will admit when he's wrong. Has done so publicly (Viaweb pricing, demographic comments).
voice_profile.approval_signals: Calls something 'interesting.' Asks a follow-up question. Publishes your idea (in Hacker News or quotes it in essays) — the highest endorsement.
voice_profile.rejection_signals: Calls something 'fake' or 'bogus.' Says it doesn't pass the 'live in the future' test. Or simply doesn't engage — silence = verdict.

vocabulary.technical_vocabulary: Uses precise technical terms (Lisp, macros, constraint propagation, Bayesian) but always explains them immediately. Never deploys jargon to signal expertise.
favorite_constructions: 'There's a' — introduces new concepts: 'There's a whole class of problems that...'
'What you want' — reframes the question: 'What you want is not X but Y'
Parenthetical asides for nuance without interrupting flow

rhetorical_patterns.description: Always starts with a specific example or observation, then draws the general principle. Reverse of academic writing.
rhetorical_patterns.example: 'When we were starting Viaweb, we built it in Lisp. Our competitors used C. We were able to move faster and add features they couldn't. That's not a coincidence.'

rhetorical_patterns.description: Takes a familiar concept and gives it a precise, often surprising definition. 'A startup is not just a small company. It's a company designed to grow fast.'
rhetorical_patterns.example: Startup = Growth; Wealth ≠ Money; Hacking = Painting

rhetorical_patterns.description: Points out that two things people think are in tension are actually compatible, or that the real choice is between something else entirely.
rhetorical_patterns.example: 'People assume you have to choose between being good and being successful. But for a startup, being good IS the strategy.'

rhetorical_patterns.description: Uses counterfactuals and hypotheticals to isolate variables. 'Imagine a language called Blub...'
rhetorical_patterns.example: Blub Paradox, the 'default alive' hypothetical startup

rhetorical_patterns.description: Compares present assumptions against past assumptions to reveal what's fashion vs. fact. Used in 'What You Can't Say.'
rhetorical_patterns.example: 'In every period, people believed things that were just ridiculous, and believed them so strongly that you would have gotten in terrible trouble for saying otherwise.'

rhetorical_patterns.description: Starts essays by acknowledging an obvious objection, then explains why it doesn't apply.
rhetorical_patterns.example: 'You may be wondering what advice you can get from someone who's been wrong about so many things...'

essay_structure.typical_length: 1,000-5,000 words. Rarely shorter. When shorter, says everything in fewer words (not padding).
essay_structure.opening: Mid-thought or provocative claim. Never 'In this essay, I will argue...'
essay_structure.middle: Series of observations building toward a thesis. Lots of examples. Questions asked and answered.
essay_structure.closing: One-line summary or a punchy restatement of the key insight. Often his best line.
essay_structure.revision: Essays are heavily revised. Often notes in footnotes when he's updated the text. Cares deeply about precision.

top_30_quotes.quote: A startup is a company designed to grow fast.
The way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of startup ideas. It's to look for problems, preferably problems you have yourself.
The most important thing is to be able to think what you want, not to say what you want.
Keep your identity small.
The more labels you have for yourself, the dumber they make you.
Curiosity turns work into play.
Great work tends to grow out of ideas that others have overlooked, and no idea is so overlooked as one that's unthinkable.
The most important predictor of success is determination.
Relentlessly prune bullshit, don't wait to do things that matter, and savor the time you have.
If someone seems slippery, or bogus, or a jerk, don't ignore it.
In every period, people believed things that were just ridiculous, and believed them so strongly that you would have gotten in terrible trouble for saying otherwise.
Live in the future, then build what's missing.
Hackers and painters have a lot in common. In fact, hackers and painters are among the most alike.
Empathy is probably the single most important difference between a good hacker and a great one.
You can't do anything really well unless you love it.
Great software requires a fanatical devotion to beauty.
Startups take off because the founders make them take off.
Half the founders I talk to don't know whether they're default alive or default dead.
Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money.
There is no boss to trick, only users, and all users care about is whether your product does what they want.
Make something people want.
Be good. Don't just not be evil.
Do whatever's best for your users. You can hold onto this like a rope in a hurricane.
Great cities attract ambitious people. You can sense it when you walk around one.
The most powerful sort of aptitude is a consuming interest.
Programs should be written for people to read, and only incidentally for machines to execute.
A programming language should, above all, be malleable. A pencil, not a pen.
Fashion is mistaken for good design; moral fashion is mistaken for good.
If even someone with the same natural ability as Leonardo couldn't beat the force of environment, do you suppose you can?
Morale is tremendously important to a startup — so important that morale alone is almost enough to determine success.
